name: Temple of Hera
description: The Temple of Hera, dedicated to the Queen of the Gods, was a sacred space of immense importance in ancient Greece. These temples, found in various locations such as Olympia, Argos, Paestum, and Samos, served as focal points for worship, festivals, and rituals honoring Hera, the goddess of marriage, women, childbirth, and family. The temples were not merely buildings, but powerful symbols of Hera's authority and the values she represented. They often housed a colossal statue of the goddess, adorned with precious materials and reflecting her regal status. Within these sacred precincts, sacrifices were offered, prayers were uttered, and elaborate ceremonies were performed, particularly during festivals like the Heraia, a games festival held in Hera's honor at Olympia. The architecture of the temples, typically Doric or Ionic in style, was designed to inspire awe and reverence, reflecting the grandeur of the goddess herself. The Temple of Hera in Olympia, one of the oldest Doric temples, was particularly significant as the site where the Olympic flame was lit, connecting the games to the divine realm. These temples were also places where important oaths were sworn, solidifying agreements under the watchful gaze of the goddess of marriage. The ongoing maintenance and embellishment of these temples demonstrated the enduring devotion of the Greek people to Hera and the vital role she played in their lives and society. The ruins of these temples continue to inspire and provide valuable insights into ancient Greek religious practices and architectural achievements, solidifying Hera's prominent place in the pantheon.
subtitle: Sacred Ground for the Queen of Gods

keyMyths.title: The Heraia Games
keyMyths.description: The Heraia was a foot race for unmarried girls, held every four years in Olympia, dedicated to Hera. The winners were awarded olive wreaths and the right to dedicate portraits of themselves in the temple.

keyMyths.title: Birth of Hephaestus
keyMyths.description: In some versions of the myth, Hera gives birth to Hephaestus without the aid of Zeus, showcasing her power. She later rejects him due to his lameness, highlighting her concern for perfection.

sources.author: Pausanias
sources.description: A detailed account of ancient Greece, including descriptions of temples and religious practices.
sources.text: A detailed account of ancient Greece, including descriptions of temples and religious practices.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A genealogical account of the Greek gods, including Hera's origins and relationships.
